### II.1 Multipolar AI pathway

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META — placement (mechanism-first), evidence, red-team.**
- Branch = no single system more impactful than all others combined. Two stories within it: **Racing** (competitive pressure to deploy — getting there) and **Structural Disempowerment** (the production web prices humans out — after). The within-branch distinguisher is the evidentiary frame: Racing is political-economy/empirical, Structural is prior-dominant.
- **Critch is placed here by mechanism, not straddling.** His mass is split *inside* the branch (~35% loss-of-control, ~50% industrial dehumanization), and he is the cleanest "number hides mechanism" case — 85% that reads like Yudkowsky but expects to dodge the singleton. Evidence: LW healthtech post; ARCHES (Critch & Krueger, 2020). Industrial dehumanization ≈ Gradual Disempowerment (Kulveit et al., 2025).
- **Carlsmith** (power-seeking, 2022) = the control problem in slow form; **Wentworth** same tradition. **Aguirre's Control Inversion** (2025) spans this and the leaf below.
- **Toner** placed here by mechanism (the danger is the competitive deployment environment); governance voice, thin — keep brief.
- **Not placed here, though often filed here:** Russell (mechanism is the control problem, fix architectural — with Bengio below) and Ord (a frame figure: century horizon, all risks). We cite their racing-adjacent statements without putting them on this branch.
:::

The branch where catastrophe needs many systems, not one. Post 2 split it three ways — competitive, cooperative, and a human variant (nations spooked by each other) — and the cooperative case, many AIs actively colluding, still has no champion. The live split is two stories that differ less in mechanism than in the kind of evidence their holders trust: how we get into trouble, and what the trouble looks like once we are in it.

**Multipolar Racing — how we get there.** Even well-meaning, rational actors produce catastrophe together: no lab can enforce a slowdown alone, so sitting out just hands the lead to someone they trust less. Anthropic frames its own existence this way; DeepMind reasons similarly. The danger is unsupervised competition — corners cut, models shipped before they are vetted, because a rival shipped first. Helen Toner is the [governance voice](https://cset.georgetown.edu/), focused on the deployment environment and the incentives that guarantee shortcuts. Two names often filed here belong elsewhere once you sort by mechanism: Stuart Russell's threat model is [the control problem](https://www.amazon.com/Human-Compatible-Artificial-Intelligence-Problem/dp/0525558616) and his fix is architectural, so he sits with Bengio below, and Toby Ord's signature is the frame — a [century-long tally across every existential risk](https://theprecipice.com/). What falsifies the racing story: durable international governance that changes the incentives, not voluntary pledges.

**Structural Disempowerment — what happens after.** The control problem without the fast takeoff. Picture an economy run by competing automated systems; no node decides to harm anyone, but the cost of swapping them back for slower human oversight climbs until human intervention is priced out, and a "production web" of firms with no incentive to protect human welfare ends up running the world. Joe Carlsmith's [power-seeking argument](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13353) is the engine — the same control problem that turns acute in the knowing optimizer below, here playing out slowly — and the [Gradual Disempowerment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.16946) paper (John Wentworth works in the same tradition) names the mechanism. Anthony Aguirre's Control Inversion argues [the race makes control of a superintelligence unattainable while the power we delegate is absorbed rather than retained](https://control-inversion.ai/), which sits across this branch and the leaf below — half "we can't control it," half "we lose it slowly." This side reasons prior-dominantly: deductions about optimization, not extrapolations from today's models.

**Andrew Critch lives on this branch, and is the sharpest proof that the number hides the mechanism.** At ~85% he reads like a hard doomer, but he expects us to *dodge* the fast single-AI takeover and die slowly instead, by industrial dehumanization, splitting his estimate [~35% to loss of control and ~50% to the production web outcompeting humans](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/Kobbt3nQgv3yn29pr/my-motivation-and-theory-of-change-for-working-in-ai). He is not standing in two places; his weight is split inside the multipolar branch. What the split shows is that he dismisses the canonical pathway, like a skeptic, then adds a worse and slower one — which puts him mechanistically nearer to LeCun in rejecting the singleton than to Yudkowsky, whose number he nearly matches.
